coredata.dc:title: Detection of trace tetracycline in fish via synchronous fluorescence quenching with carbon quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted silica
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ji
Lin, Zheng-Zhong
Nur, A-Zha
Lu, Yan
Wu, Ming-Hui
Zeng, Jun
Chen, Xiao-Mei
Huang, Zhi-Yong
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence-based sensor combining synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy (SFS) with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) was fabricated with reverse microemulsion method. Tetracycline (TC), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and carbon quantum dots (CDs) were used as template, functional monomer, cross-linker and signal sources respectively in the probe preparation. A synchronous fluorescence emission (λem) at 355nm was observed for the prepared MIP-coated CDs (MIP@CDs) particles when the wavelength interval (Δλ) was set as 70nm, and the synchronous fluorescence intensity could be rapidly and efficiently quenched by TC based on inner filter effect (IFE). The quenching efficiencies of synchronous fluorescence intensity was linearly fitted with tetracycline (TC) concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 50μmolL−1 with a detection limit (DL) of 9nmolL−1 (3σ, n=9). The MIP@CDs was used as a probe to detect TC in fish samples with the recoveries ranging from 98.4% to 103.1% and the relative standard deviation less than 6.0%. The results illustrated that the as-prepared MIP@CDs could be applied to the detection of trace TC in fish samples with rapidity, high sensitivity and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Inner filter effect
Tetracycline
Fish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TC in Fish Samples
Synchronous Fluorescence Spectra and Quenching Mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of MIP@CDs sensor and the recognition mechanism via IFE quenching.

Synchronous fluorescence spectra (at 355nm) of NIP@CDs (a) and MIP@CDs after (b) and before (c) removal of templates. Inset shows the appearance of MIP@CDs in water under daylight (A) and UV (365nm) light (B).
TEM images of CDs (a), MIP@CDs (b&d) and NIP@CDs (c&e). Insets show the distribution of polymeric diameters.
FT-IR spectra of MIP@QDs and NIP@QDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of MIP@CDs, TC, the mixture of MIP@CDs and TC (a); the synchronous fluorescence spectrum (355nm) of MIP@CDs and the UV absorption spectrum of TC (b); the fluorescence decay profiles of MIP@CDs in the absence and presence of 10μmolL−1 TC (c).

Synchronous fluorescence spectra of MIP@CDs with the addition of TC at different concentrations (a). The relationship between F
0
/F of MIP@CDs and the concentrations of TC (b). F and F 0 were the synchronous fluorescence intensities of MIP@CDs with and without addition of TC respectively.
Quenching effects of synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs to CAP, OTC, CTC and TC in water (each of the concentrations was 5μmolL−1).
The comparison of tetracycline detection with different methods.
Recoveries of TC in fish samples via SFS.

head.ce:title: Detection of trace tetracycline in fish via synchronous fluorescence quenching with carbon quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted silica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Collaborative Innovation Center for Exploitation and Utilization of Marine Biological Resources, Xiamen 361102, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Collaborative Innovation Center for Exploitation and Utilization of Marine Biological Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence-based sensor combining synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy (SFS) with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) was fabricated with reverse microemulsion method. Tetracycline (TC), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and carbon quantum dots (CDs) were used as template, functional monomer, cross-linker and signal sources respectively in the probe preparation. A synchronous fluorescence emission (λem) at 355nm was observed for the prepared MIP-coated CDs (MIP@CDs) particles when the wavelength interval (Δλ) was set as 70nm, and the synchronous fluorescence intensity could be rapidly and efficiently quenched by TC based on inner filter effect (IFE). The quenching efficiencies of synchronous fluorescence intensity was linearly fitted with tetracycline (TC) concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 50μmolL−1 with a detection limit (DL) of 9nmolL−1 (3σ, n=9). The MIP@CDs was used as a probe to detect TC in fish samples with the recoveries ranging from 98.4% to 103.1% and the relative standard deviation less than 6.0%. The results illustrated that the as-prepared MIP@CDs could be applied to the detection of trace TC in fish samples with rapidity, high sensitivity and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that tetracycline (TC) as a familiar antibiotic can effectively inhibit the infections from gram positive and negative bacteria, rickettsia, ultravirus, spirochaeta and parasite, etc. [1]. TC has been used to cure the diseases caused by bacterial and pathogenic microbe infections in fishery [2–3]. However, the excess usages of TC may pose a significant threat to human health [4]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a novel and flexible method to detect TC in fish rapidly and accurately.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attentions due to the advantages of simple preparation, low cost, low toxicity, high quantum yield, good water-solubility, chemical inertness, and resistance to photobleaching [5–6]. As an electron acceptor or donor, CDs was widely applied to photoelectricity, catalysis and analytical sensors because of its chemical fluorescence and electrochemical fluorescence [7–8]. Many elements have been used to improve the performance of CDs by doping or functionalizing. N-doping is one of the most used elements to enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs. For example, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) has been synthesized using anhydrous citric acid and urea as carbon source and nitrogen source respectively with a quantum yield of 42.5% at λem of 440nm [6].

Synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy (SFS) proposed by Lloyd in 1971 [9] presents many merits including simplified spectra, high selectivity and sensitivity, less interference from light scattering compared with normal fluorescence spectroscopy [10]. SFS can be achieved by setting constant wavelength interval, constant energy interval, variable angle and matrix isopotential according to different scanning modes [11–12], in which constant wavelength interval is mostly used [13–14]. SFS have been achieved by heavy metal based semiconductor quantum dots. For example, CdS QDs was used to detect mercury and silver ions in water samples based on the SFS method [15–16]. A denatured ovalbumin coated CdTe QDs was used as a nanoprobe to detect mercury ion through SFS [17]. CdTe QDs coated with a molecular imprinting silica has been applied to the detection of leucomalachite green (LMG) in fish samples based on the quenching effect of synchronous fluorescence with high selectivity and accuracy [18]. However, heavy metal based QDs present high toxicity to human body and environment [19]. Therefore, CDs based fluorescence sensors have attracted much attention.

However, the CDs fluorescence sensors lacked specificity. Recently, many studies combining CDs and molecular imprinting technique have been conducted. For example, a mesoporous structure of MIPs@CDs was synthesized as a fluorescence sensor and has been successfully used to selectively detect TNT in soil and water samples [20]. Similarly, several MIPs@CDs have also been prepared and applied to the selective detection of bisphenol A [21], sterigmatocystin [22] and α-amanitin [23] with detection limits (DLs) of 30nmolL−1, 58nmolL−1 and 33nmolL−1 respectively.

In the present study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots were encapsulated with molecularly imprinting silica to form the fluorescence MIP@CDs nanoparticles. With the quenching effect of the synchronous fluorescence by TC based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the MIP@CDs was used as a sensor to detect TC in fish samples.

TC (tetracycline), CAP (chloramphenicol), CTC (chlorotetracycline), OTC (oxytetracycline), n-hexanol, triton X-100, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous citric, urea were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NH3·H2O, KBr, cyclohexane, acetone, ethanol, methanol and hydrochloric acid were produced from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All chemical reagents throughout the experiment were analytical reagent grade and were used without any purification. The water in the experiment was obtained from a TKA ultra water purification system (Germany, 18.2MΩ·cm).

Synchronous fluorescence spectra were recorded with a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA). Fluorescence lifetime measurement was performed with a FLS 980-STM steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh instruments Inc., UK). The pH value of buffer solutions was measured using a pH510m (Eutech Instruments Pte. Ltd., Singapore). Absorption spectra were detected via a Lambda 265 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed in a Nicolet 740SX spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., US). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed with a JEM-2100 microscope (Japan Electorn Optics Laboratory, Japan).

The preparation of CDs was performed according to our previous report with some modification [6]. Typically, 0.5g anhydrous citric and 0.5g urea were ultrasonically dissolved in 10mL water, and was sealed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and heated at 160°C for 4h. After cooling to room temperature, a dark green solution was obtained. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots precipitated with ethanol-acetone (1:1, v/v) was obtained by drying under vacuum for further use.

The MIP@CDs was fabricated via the reverse microemulsion method with some modification [24]. Briefly, 7.5mL of cyclohexane, 1.8mL of n-hexanol and 1.77mL of triton X-100 were sequentially added into 100mL-flask and magnetically stirred at 800rpm for 20min at room temperature. Then, 2mg CDs powder dispersed in 1mL water was added into the mixture. After stirring for another 10min, 0.6mmol TEOS (crosslinking agent) and 60μL ammonium hydroxide (initiator) were added in sequence. The mixture was sealed and stirred for 2h. A pre-polymerization solution consisting of 0.04mmol TC and 0.12mmol APTES that was kept at 4°C for 2h, was added. Finally, the mixture was stirred in the dark for 24h. After polymerization, 10mL acetone was injected into the flask. The final product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with methanol under ultrasonic agitation to remove the templates of TC until no TC was detected at 355nm with UV–vis. The MIP@CDs was dried under vacuum. As a control, the non-imprinted polymer-coated CDs (NIP@CDs) was synthesized following the same procedure but without the addition of TC templates.

Copper grids coated with an amorphous carbon film were respectively submerged into a suspension of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs in ethanol. After 3s, the copper grids were dried under vacuum at 60°C for 4h. The TEM images were recorded on a JEM-2100 microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV.
The tablets were prepared by mixing the ready-made polymer powders (MIP@CDs or NIP@CDs) with KBr at 1:100 (w/w), and the FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 740SX FT-IR spectrometer.

MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs were separately dispersed in water at a concentration of 0.005mgmL−1 for synchronous and normal fluorescence measurement. Both of the excitation and emission slits were 5nm. The synchronous fluorescence spectra were recorded at different wavelength intervals (Δλ), and the normal fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength (λex) of 340nm.

The fluorescence lifetimes of MIP@CDs were measured in the absence and presence of 10μmolL−1 TC respectively. The data were fitted with the following double exponential equation [25]:
I
t
= B 1 ⋅ exp
− t / τ 1
+ B 2 ⋅ exp
− t / τ 2
where I(t) was the fluorescence intensity of MIP@CDs at the time of t; τ 1 and τ 2 were the long and short fluorescence lifetimes of MIP@CDs, respectively. B 1 and B 2 were the statistical weight correspondingly [26]. The average fluorescence lifetime (τ
av ) was calculated by the following formula [27]:
τ av =
∑ B i ⋅
τ i 2
∑ B i ⋅ τ i
where τ
i was the measured lifetime and B
i was the value of statistical weight.

50mg of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs was respectively dispersed in 4mL water containing TC at different concentrations at room temperature. After adsorption and centrifugation, the adsorption capacities of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs could be calculated according to the following equation [28].
Q =
M ⋅ V ⋅
C 1 − C 2
m
where Q (μgg−1) was the adsorption capacities of MIP@CDs or NIP@CDs. M (gmol−1) was the absolute molecular mass of TC. V (L) was the volume of adsorption system. C 1 and C 2 (μmolL−1) were the initial and final concentrations of TC in supernatants, respectively. m (g) was the mass of MIP@CDs or NIP@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in Fish Samples
ce:section.ce:para: TC in fish samples was extracted according to previous reports with some modification [18,29]. Briefly, 1g skinless fish was mixed with 5mL acetonitrile. After homogenization for 10min, the mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was diluted to 250mL with water. 6mL of the supernatants was spiked with TC at the concentrations of 0.5, 5 and 10μmolL−1, respectively. Then, 0.005mL of MIP@CDs (6.0mgmL−1 in water) was added and mixed for 5min. The intensities of synchronous fluorescence (F) were measured at 355nm with a wavelength interval (Δλ) of 70nm. Similarly, F 0 values were also measured in the absence of TC whereby the quenching efficiencies (F 0 / F) of SFS were calculated.

The preparation route of MIP@CDs was shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, template (TC) and functional monomer (APTES) were spontaneously assembled through hydrogen bonds. After the addition of cross-linking agent (TEOS), the MIP@CDs nanoparticles were synthesized and the CDs entrapped in the particles played the role of fluorescence source. The synchronous fluorescence spectra (at 355nm) of MIP@CDs before and after the removal of templates were recorded. Fig. 2 showed that the synchronous fluorescence intensity of MIP@CDs was 36% of the NIP@CDs before the removal of TC, but the intensity almost recovered (90%) after removal. The result indicated that most of the TC molecules in the specific cavities of MIP@CDs could be washed out. In addition, the appearance of MIP@CDs showed colorless under daylight and a bright blue fluorescence emission under UV light could be observed as shown in the inset photograph in Fig. 2.

The TEM images of CDs, MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs were recorded as shown in Fig. 3 . The average diameter of CDs was about 3nm (Fig. 3a), and the average diameters of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs were about 75nm (Fig. 3b & c), respectively. The polymer particles were in regular spherical shape with several CDs compactly entrapped in the centre of each particle (red circles in Fig. 3d & e).

The FT-IR spectra of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs were shown in Fig. 4 . The peaks at 3200cm−1 (NH stretching vibration), 1574cm−1 and 1556cm−1 (NH bending vibration) suggested the presence of APTES [30]. The peaks at 1384cm−1 (CH bending vibration) confirmed the presence of APTES and TEOS [31]. The peaks at 1065cm−1 (SiOSi asymmetric stretching vibration), 793cm−1 (SiOSi symmetrical stretching vibration) and 462cm−1 (SiOSi bending vibration) indicated the presence of TEOS [24]. After elution, the FT-IR spectrum of MIP@CDs was similar to that of NIP@CDs, indicating that most of the template molecules had been removed.

The effects of synthesis conditions of MIP@CDs on the synchronous fluorescence intensities and quenching efficiencies were shown in Fig. S1. The optimum molar ratio of TC to APTES and TEOS was 1:3:15. The optimum amount of CDs was 2mg. In addition, the concentration of MIP@CDs and pH values in detection system were also optimized (Fig. S2). The results showed that 0.005mgmL−1 of MIP@CDs in Tris-HCl buffer (pH7.0) resulted in a strong synchronous fluorescence and high quenching efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synchronous Fluorescence Spectra and Quenching Mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The synchronous fluorescence spectra of MIP@CDs with wavelength intervals (Δλ) ranging from 20nm to 400nm were recorded as shown in Fig. S3. A phenomenon of blue shift was observed with the increases of wavelength interval. A strong synchronous fluorescence emission at 355nm was achieved at a Δλ of as 70nm.

The quenching mechanism of MIP@CDs toward TC was also investigated according to the results in Fig. 5 . The UV–Vis absorption spectra of MIP@CDs, TC, and the mixture of MIP@CDs and TC were shown in Fig. 5a. No additional absorbance was observed after mixing MIP@CDs with TC, indicating that the binding of TC on the surface of MIP@CDs was attributed to physical adsorption [29]. The synchronous fluorescence emission of MIP@CDs at 355nm was completely overlapped by the UV absorption of TC as shown in Fig. 5b, which interpreted the quenching effect of the synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs by TC. The result in Fig. 5c showed that the fluorescence lifetime of MIP@CDs was 7.95ns. After mixing with TC, the fluorescence lifetime (7.81ns) remained almost unchanged. It could be deduced that the fluorescence quenching was caused by IFE instead of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) because an obvious change of fluorescence lifetime would be observed for FRET [32].

The stabilities of CDs, MIP@CDs, NIP@CDs, and the quenching response time of MIP@CDs toward TC were tested as shown in Fig. S4. The synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs remained almost unchanged after the storage of 7days, while the synchronous fluorescence of CDs gradually declined (Fig. S4a). This fact indicated that the silica layer could protect CDs nucleus from fluorescence bleaching [33]. After the addition of TC, the synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs was stable (Fig. S4b) after 3min, which exhibited a rapid fluorescence response.

The isothermal adsorption experiments of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs were carried out by separately mixing 50mg of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs with TC at different concentrations [34]. As shown in Fig. S5a, both of the adsorption capacities of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs increased with the increases of initial TC concentrations. The maximal adsorption capacities of MIP@CDs and NIP@CDs were 535 and 467μgg−1, respectively, indicating the presence of specific cavities on the surface of MIP@QDs [35].

The adsorption kinetics as shown in Fig. S5b showed that the adsorption equilibration time was no more than 5min, which was in consistent with the response time of synchronous fluorescence (Fig. S4b). The results indicated that the adsorption of TC on the surface of MIP@CDs was rapid and was further resulted in effective synchronous fluorescence quenching based on inner filter effect.

The synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs could be effectively quenched by TC at different concentrations as shown in Fig. 6a. The quenching efficiencies were fitted by the following two linear equations as shown in Fig. 6b, in which the equation of F 0/F =0.127C 1 +0.999 (R 2 =0.994) was suitable for low level (0.1–1μmolL−1), while F 0 / F =0.088C 2 +1.038 (R 2 =0.997) was fitted for high level (1–50μmolL−1). The occurrence of two linear relationships might be due to the heterogeneity of the specific cavities in MIP@CDs nanoparticles [18,33]. The detection limit based on 3σ / K (n=9) was calculated to be 9nmolL−1, less than the one of 19nmolL−1 via normal fluorescence quenching based on our measurement. Furthermore, the detection limit by the SFS method was far below the limitation (225nmolL−1) of TC residue in aquatic products set in China (NY 5070-2002). The analytical indexes of the present study were compared with those of the typical results in previous reports. The comparison results in Table 1 showed that the linear range and detection limit of MIP@CDs sensor in this work was superior to those of the MoS2 QDs sensor and MIP@CdTe QDs on glass, but was inferior to those of the HPLC and electrochemistry methods. In addition, the detection methods by the optical sensors showed rapid, simple and convenient compared with the HPLC and electrochemistry. Therefore, the MIP@CDs could be used as a novel fluorescence sensor for the rapid detection of TC in samples.

Chloramphenicol (CAP) that might be illegally used in aquaculture, and two chemical analogues of TC, oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlorotetracycline (CTC), were used for the selectivity tests [36]. The maximum UV–Vis absorption peaks of CAP and CTC located at 275nm and 365nm, respectively. Two absorption peaks were observed for TC (260nm and 355nm) and OTC (260nm and 350nm), respectively. Result in Fig. 7 showed that CTC and OTC also exhibited quenching effects due to the overlap between UV absorption peaks and synchronous fluorescence emission [18], but the quenching efficiencies were obviously lower than that of TC. CAP exhibited the lowest quenching effect because its absorption was far from the synchronous fluorescence emission. The result indicated that the MIP@CDs sensor exhibited specific recognition for TC [20,24].

The fluorescence sensor of MIP@CDs was applied to the detection of trace TC in fish samples. Results in Table 2 showed that the recoveries in the spiked samples ranged from 98.4% to 103.1% with RSDs less than 6.0%. Therefore, the as-prepared MIP@CDs could be used as a fluorescence sensor to detect trace TC rapidly in fish samples.

A novel synchronous fluorescence sensor of MIP@CDs was successfully synthesized with a reverse microemulsion method. The synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs at 355nm was achieved at Δλ of 70nm, and was completely overlapped by the absorption of TC, which caused the maximum synchronous fluorescence quenching due to IFE. The synchronous fluorescence of MIP@CDs could be linearly quenched by TC ranging from 0.1 to 50μmolL−1 with a detection limit of 9nmolL−1. The results demonstrated that the as-prepared MIP@CDs could be used as a sensor to detect trace amounts of TC rapidly in fish samples.

This research was supported by the Foundation from the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China (2016Y0064), the opening project of Fujian Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center of Marine Functional Food (C11172), Innovative Research Team of Jimei University, China (2010A007), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2015J01615, 2017J01633), and the National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (201610390042, 201710390022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.09.066.
